does: This paper introduces AVISE, a modular open-source framework designed to systematically identify vulnerabilities and evaluate the security of AI systems and models. The core contribution extends theory-of-mind-based multi-turn Red Queen attacks into an Adversarial Language Model (ALM) augmented variant, paired with an automated Security Evaluation Test (SET) comprising 25 test cases for discovering jailbreak vulnerabilities in language models. The framework employs an Evaluation Language Model (ELM) that classifies whether test cases successfully jailbreak target models, achieving 92% accuracy with an F1-score of 0.91 and Matthews correlation coefficient of 0.83. The authors demonstrate the framework by evaluating nine recently released language models of varying sizes, establishing a concrete methodology for comparative security assessment across different model architectures and capacities.
connects: This work builds directly on prior research in adversarial robustness and language model security, particularly extending theory-of-mind-based attack methodologies that have roots in cognitive science applications to adversarial settings. It responds to the critical gap in systematic vulnerability evaluation frameworks, building on earlier work in red-teaming and adversarial testing of neural systems. The paper connects to research on jailbreaking language models and prompt injection attacks, synthesizing these into a unified evaluation methodology. It also draws on the broader literature of AI safety evaluation frameworks and adversarial machine learning, which has increasingly focused on multi-turn and contextual attack strategies as models become more sophisticated at single-turn defense.
enables: This framework enables systematic, reproducible security assessment of language models across development cycles and model releases, supporting more rigorous safety benchmarking in the field. By automating vulnerability discovery through SET and ELM components, the work points toward scalable red-teaming infrastructure that could be integrated into model development pipelines. Future directions include extending the framework to other AI modalities beyond language models, developing adaptive test cases that evolve with improving defenses, and using AVISE to inform architectural changes and training procedures that enhance robustness. The modular design suggests pathways toward standardized security evaluation metrics, facilitating comparison across organizations and supporting regulatory requirements for AI deployment in critical domains.
